{{wikidata link|Q18339461}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} |社格= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=myojin-taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|WD=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|WD=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|WD=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|WD=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|WD=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|WD=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|WD=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|WD=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|WD=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|WD=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|WD=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. [[File:蜷城の獅子舞.JPG|thumb|Ninagi shishimai]] === References === * Fukuoka Prefecture Shrine Records * Village History: Hinashiro * Amagi City History === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === *[http://minagijinjya.com Minagi Shrine (Hayashida) Official Site] {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:福岡県の神社]] [[Category:朝倉市の歴史]] [[Category:朝倉市の建築物]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:筑前国の式内社|名みなきしんしや]] [[Category:福岡県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]]

{{wikidata link|Q18339461}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} |社格= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=myojin-taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|WD=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|WD=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|WD=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|WD=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|WD=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|WD=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|WD=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|WD=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|WD=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|WD=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|WD=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. [[File:蜷城の獅子舞.JPG|thumb|Ninagi shishimai]] === References === * Fukuoka Prefecture Shrine Records * Village History: Hinashiro * Amagi City History === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === *[http://minagijinjya.com Minagi Shrine (Hayashida) Official Site] {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:朝倉市の歴史]] [[Category:朝倉市の建築物]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:筑前国の式内社|名みなきしんしや]] [[Category:福岡県の旧県社]][[Category:qq]]

{{wikidata link|Q18339461}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} |社格= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=myojin-taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|WD=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|WD=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|WD=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|WD=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|WD=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|WD=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|WD=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|WD=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|WD=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|WD=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|WD=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|WD=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|WD=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|WD=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|WD=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|WD=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|WD=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|WD=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|WD=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|WD=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|WD=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|WD=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{wikidata link|Q18339461}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|WD=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|WD=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|WD=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|WD=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|WD=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|WD=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|WD=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|WD=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|WD=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|WD=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|WD=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|WD=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|WD=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|WD=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|WD=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|WD=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|WD=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|WD=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. (朝倉市林田)]] {{wikidata link|Q18339461}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|qid=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611}} |社格= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=myojin-taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|qid=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|qid=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|qid=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|qid=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|qid=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|qid=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|qid=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|qid=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|qid=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|qid=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|qid=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|qid=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|qid=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|qid=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|qid=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|qid=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|qid=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|qid=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|qid=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}. [[File:蜷城の獅子舞.JPG|thumb|Ninagi shishimai]] === References === * Fukuoka Prefecture Shrine Records * Village History: Hinashiro * Amagi City History === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === *[http://minagijinjya.com Minagi Shrine (Hayashida) Official Site] {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Minagi Shrine]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:朝倉市の歴史]] [[Category:朝倉市の建築物]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Chikuzen Province]] [[Category:福岡県の旧県社]]

(朝倉市林田)]] {{wikidata link|Q18339461|ja|美奈宜神社 (朝倉市林田)}} == Japanese content == {{参照方法|date=2014年7月7日 (月) 13:36 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称= Minagi Shrine |画像= [[File:美奈宜神社.JPG|260px|Minagi Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地= {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|qid=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}} Hayashida 210 |位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611}} |社格= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=myojin-taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} |創建= {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} regency year 2 ({{ill|lt=202|Q30333|qid=Q30333|1=202}}) |例祭= {{ill|lt=October 21|Q2960|qid=Q2960|1=October 21}} (Okunchi) |神事= Shishimai of Ninagi (Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|lt=intangible cultural property|Q2901957|qid=Q2901957|1=Intangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukuoka|label=Minagi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Minagi Shrine''' (みなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hayashida, {{ill|lt=Asakura|Q720625|qid=Q720625|1=Asakura, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. === Enshrined deities === About 1,800 years ago, following the instruction of her father {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}} campaigned against {{ill|lt=Kumaso|Q1196897|qid=Q1196897|1=Kumaso}} with the empress, but died of illness. The empress concealed this and planned an overseas campaign against the main enemy {{ill|lt=Silla|Q28456|qid=Q28456|1=Silla}}, gathered troops, prepared ships and arms, and prayed to the gods before launching what is said to be Japan's first foreign expedition from Nago-ya in Hizen. During the voyage she prayed for victory to three deities: {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōanamuji-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611}}. The voyage was safe and the ships reached Silla, where war began. After consecutive victories and surrender under three terms, they returned triumphantly to Takahashi-no-tsu in Hizen. Thereafter, the three deities to whom victory had been prayed were enshrined; they are the three deities of Minagi Shrine. === History === === Founding === In Empress Jingu regency year 2 (202), when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} visited Kyushu, villagers asked her to subdue an evil man called Hagurokumawashi who lived in the mountain at Kokoro. The empress prayed, and received an oracle: "Use this ebb jewel to dry up the river, ask the river dragon, build a fortress in one night, then use the flood jewel to fill the water at once and destroy him by flood attack." After this, the village became peaceful. Wishing to build a shrine for the deity, she released a white egret. It flew along the {{ill|lt=Chikugo River|Q954320|qid=Q954320|1=Chikugo River}}, then descended where clear water welled up. The shrine was built nearby. The place name Ninagi (蜷城) was written as Minagi (美奈宜), and the shrine was named Minagi Shrine. The village protected by the river dragon was called Ninashiro, later becoming Hinashiro; the White Egret Mound remains today at Kataenji Shiratori 1. === Rank as myojin-taisha === In the Nara period, when affairs of state occurred, envoys worshiped notable shrines nationwide ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}). At that time there were 204 such shrines (285 deity seats). According to {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|qid=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, after the king of Balhae offered tribute to the Nara court in September {{ill|lt=730|Q23632|qid=Q23632|1=Massiola}}, the court held a myojin festival in October and dedicated part of the tribute to shrines. In Kyushu proper, the following 12 shrines received offerings and were included: * Chikuzen: {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|qid=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|qid=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine|qid=Q11390939}}, {{ill|lt=Shikaumi Shrine|Q11491171|qid=Q11491171|1=Shikaumi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Minagi Shrine|Q17503178|qid=Q17503178|1=Minagi Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Chikushi Shrine|Q11602932|qid=Q11602932|1=Chikushi Shrine}}, Tatose Shrine (7 shrines) * Chikugo: Takara Tamataru-no-Mikoto Shrine, Toyohime Shrine (2 shrines) * Hizen: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tashima-za Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (1 shrine) * Higo: Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Aso Shrine|Q715639|qid=Q715639|1=Aso Shrine}}) (1 shrine) * Buzen: Yawata-hime Shrine (present {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|qid=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}}) (1 shrine) Only these 12 received envoy worship each time myojin rites were held. In four provinces (Bungo, Hyuga, Satsuma, and one additional province; present Kagoshima and Miyazaki), no shrines were listed. As noted above, Minagi Shrine received the honor of myojin rites in the Nara and Heian periods. === Medieval period === According to the ''Sandai Jitsuroku'', in Seiwa Tenno Jogan 1 (859), first month 27th day, Minagi Shrine in Chikuzen was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fifth Rank Upper. In {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} established in Encho 5 (927), Minagi Shrine was listed as a major Engishiki shrine in the myojin register. That Minagi Shrine appears in multiple state histories as a famous shrine of Kyushu indicates the Chikugo River supported ancient life, settlements formed on both banks and developed, and this river was a major route crossing Kyushu. Minagi Shrine, enshrined by the river, drew deep devotion from many communities on both banks and many travelers. In Engen 2 (1338), during fighting at the Mizushiro crossing of the Chikugo River between forces of Kikuchi Taketoshi and an advance unit of {{ill|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|Q297107|qid=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the shrine suffered war fire and lost all treasures and records, declining afterward; Takauji, fearing divine punishment, rebuilt the shrine. In Eisho 6 (1509), Akizuki castle lord Akizuki Tanetoki rebuilt shrine buildings and dedicated a mikoshi and purification standards, which continue to lead the procession to this day. === Early modern period === In Keicho 6 (1601), first lord of Fukuoka domain {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|qid=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}} greatly revered the shrine, sponsored construction, and designated it the head shrine of the lower district. In Genroku 15 (1702), the current sacred gate was built. In Genroku 17 (1704), the current stone torii was dedicated, with pillar inscriptions written by Kaibara Ekiken's wife, the Egashima family. In Koka 4 (1847), the 1,650th anniversary was held. In Bunsei 11 (1823), the current shrine buildings were renovated by Fukuoka domain chief retainer Kuroda Harima. === Present day === In Meiji 30 (1897), it was promoted to prefectural shrine rank. In Showa 28 (1953), it left state management and became Religious Corporation Minagi Shrine, as at present. The Ninagi Shishimai, continued since the Edo period, is designated an intangible cultural property of Fukuoka Prefecture. === Festivals === * January 1: New Year festival * April 29: Spring festival * July 30: Summer purification festival (Oyodo) * October 17: Jinkasai * October 21: Okunchi (autumn grand festival and divine procession) * Last Sunday of November: Niiname festival === Cultural property === === Ninagi shishimai === This lion dance serves in the divine procession of Minagi Shrine held every October 21, and consists of two male-female pairs of lion heads. It is distinctive in that palm fiber ({{ill|lt=shuro|Q157364|qid=Q157364|1=Trachycarpus}}) is used for the body and even for bindings on dancers' legs. The lions are dedicated from the Nagata and Uonoki districts. Nagata guards the procession mikoshi, and Uonoki carries the mikoshi platform. Unlike many kagura-lineage lion dances in Chikuzen, this one includes no accompanying kagura and has fewer theatrical elements. Also, the lion form as a ritual for warding illness and praying for good harvest is well preserved. Dances of this lineage are transmitted across the middle Chikugo basin, but only Ninagi preserves a fully traditional form to today. In April 1976 (Showa 51), it was designated a Fukuoka Prefecture {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=intangible folk cultural property|1=UNKNOWN}}.
